r  Any software  that  reaches  the European market has  to
a
adhere to the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) [1],
known as  the  toughest  privacy and security  law in  the
world [2]. Moreover, with the EU’s Cyber Resilience Act
(CRA)  [3] on the horizon,  software developers will  soon
face the challenge of writing code that complies with stricter
security and privacy standards. These regulations extend to
mobile applications that gather data from users in the EU.
The GDPR defines personal data as any information relating
to an identified or identifiable natural person, a data subject,
and imposes obligations on the access, storage and processing
of such data. Under the GDPR, data protection violations can
result in severe financial penalties [4]. If these violations cause
vulnerabilities and data leaks, additional fines may be levied
under  the CRA. Companies  thus must  identify and secure

security-critical data-processing code,  and prioritize  testing
efforts accordingly. The main challenge  is  identifying this
sensitive code early in the development lifecycle.
The growing demand for privacy by design [5], both by
end users and by the GDPR, necessitates that app developers
use state-of-the-art technical measures to protect their users’
privacy. The legal description of the GDPR is very complex
and lengthy and hence it can be difficult for app developers
to understand. Since they lack legal expertise, app developers
may be left wondering which technical measures need to be
taken for which categories of user  input data.  In Android,
merely including data-hungry third-party libraries may result
in inadvertent data sharing [6], highlighting the importance of
careful consideration in app development.
Advocating for transparency, Article §13 [7] of the GDPR
mandates that the collection and processing of personal data
be disclosed to the user through documents such as privacy
policies. Therefore, all Android apps must provide a privacy
policy which explains how apps collect, share, and process
user data. However, these privacy policies are often long and
vague, and may not always be authored by the app developers.
Several studies [8]–[12] have consistently shown significant
discrepancies between privacy policies and the actual source
code, undermining their accuracy and misleading users.
To address  these inaccuracies, Google recently launched
the data safety section [13], which shifts the responsibility of
privacy-relevant reporting to app developers. This necessitates
the completion of a form, outlining how apps collect, share,
and secure user data. App developers also need to share if
the app uses data protection measures such as encryption or
on-demand data deletion. Before being able to create a store
listing for an Android app, developers must complete this
form. This manual process often results in inaccuracies in re-
porting [14], [15], providing users with a false sense of privacy.
To bridge the gap between reported and actual handling of
privacy-relevant data, app developers need technical support.
To address this,  this paper presents an approach that uses

We next use a motivating example to define and explain our
problem statement.

A. Motivating Example
Alice,  a Java programmer unfamiliar with legal privacy
frameworks, needs to create a Google Play Store [21] listing
for her company’s app. To accomplish this  task,  she must
complete the data safety form on Play Console [22]. This step
is mandatory for creating the store listing.
Yet, Alice has no prior knowledge regarding privacy or
GDPR requirements. She thus consults Play Console Help [23]
to understand how to complete  the form,  and learns  that
she needs to also report data collected by the app but sent
directly to analytics engines. Despite knowing that the app uses
Google Analytics [24], she struggles to identify which specific
analytics APIs collect which data from the app code. The app’s
privacy policy is outdated, offering little to no guidance.
Google rejects her  form request,  and insists on getting
more explanations about data collection and sharing. Alice
then consults colleagues, but they struggle to understand the
complicated terminology used in Google’s guide [25]. After
days of manual effort, Alice finally completes the data safety
form and uploads the app on the Play Store. Yet, she still
has no way to make sure that the data she entered is actually
correct, that it corresponds fully to the app’s implementation.
Alice’s uncertainty worries Bob, the newly appointed Data
Protection Officer (DPO) [26], who knows about  the severe
fines imposed by the GDPR and CRA. Bob initiates a data
protection impact assessment  (DPIA)  [27]  to comply with
Article §35 [28] of  the GDPR. This process would require
Alice and Bob to use the sample DPIA template [29] and ad-
dress questions regarding data collection, processing, storage,
sharing, deletion, and pseudonymization. With no tool support
to answer such specific questions, they are forced to manually
sift through extensive lines of code to find answers.
Despite Alice’s expertise as a programmer,  the codebase
might be too large for her to manually locate the necessary
information. She may need to randomly sample the source
code and check  it manually. However, Bob might  doubt
whether the sampled code sections include all sections needed
to comprehensively answer  the DPIA questions. This could
result  in  lengthy discussions with  the  legal  team,  during
which Alice would need to explain complex technical concepts
to non-technical stakeholders. Consequently, completing the
DPIA could require weeks of manual effort, particularly for
start-ups that have not yet hired a DPO.
As part of a separate study, we conducted interviews with
DPOs and privacy experts, which confirmed that these chal-
lenges are widespread. One DPO highlighted the disconnect
between legal and technical  teams: “Ideally, developers and
legal  teams should collaborate and conduct a DPIA before
starting processing activities. But as my professional experi-
ence is concerned, that is the biggest void in the market. There
is no communication between developers and lawyers—they
are  in two completely different worlds. Privacy by design
requires developers to consider legal principles, but this rarely
happens.” A privacy researcher further emphasized the gap:
“According to GDPR, DPOs should maintain the record of

We next discuss existing research on this topic.
Program slicing.  Jicer  [39]  is  the only available static
slicer  for Android apps, which uses Soot  [20]’s DotGraph
API [40] to generate static graphs in SVG format. However,
these graphs do not scale well and lack interactivity. Xu et al.’s
survey [41] provides a broad overview of slicing techniques
and challenges. In 2007, Sridharan et al. [18] proposed thin
slicing, which excludes control dependencies and focuses only

WALA [42]. To the best of our knowledge, our approach is the
first to use static program slicing to bridge the gap between
technical and legal aspects of Android data protection.
Assessment  of  data  protection  requirements.  PTP-
Droid [12] uses the taint analysis tool FlowDroid [43] to study
the reachability of third party libraries and compares it with
the privacy policy section of data sharing with third parties.
Similarly, ATPChecker [44] uses FlowDroid to automatically
identify whether  the usage of  in-app  third-party  libraries
complies with privacy-relevant regulations. PTPDroid and AT-
PChecker focus on data sharing and lack examination of data
processing. In contrast, our approach aims to conduct a more
holistic privacy analysis by highlighting where personal data
is disguised and processed. Tang and Østvold [45] describe an
automatic software analysis technique that presents the results
as a graph of privacy flows and operations. Their analysis
aims to assist app developers and DPOs in evaluating privacy
compliance and simplifying DPIAs. While our approach has
similar objectives, we intend to use static program slicing and
further conduct a risk-based assessment of collected data.
Data safety section. Researchers have studied Android’s
privacy labels since Google Play [13]  introduced the data
safety section. A Mozilla study [38] graded popular apps
based on discrepancies between data  safety sections  and
privacy policies. Khandelwal et al. [14] noted underreporting,
overreporting, and inconsistencies in data practices, analyzing
developer interactions using snapshot data without source code
analysis. In contrast, we aim to analyze source code to help
developers report information accurately.
In 2022, Google introduced Checks [46], a paid service
that  assists app developers  in completing  the data safety
section. However, its cost could hinder many app developers
from using its services. An open source alternative to this is
Matcha [47], a JetBrain’s plugin aiding developers in creating
accurate Google play data safety labels. Matcha analyzes the
source code and suggests labels to the developers, who can
then annotate the source code. Unlike Matcha, our approach
does not need developers to annotate their source code and
extends beyond data safety sections to help developers identify
privacy-relevant data in the source code.
A recent experiment conducted by Khedkar et al. [15] has
revealed discrepancies between the data collection reported in
data safety sections and the source code of  the app. Their
approach introduces a prototype to statically detect and label
the privacy-relevant data collected by the source code, user
interface screens, and permissions. Their findings highlight the
need for improved tool support to aid Android developers in
comprehensively understanding privacy implications in their
source code—a gap that we aim to address with SliceViz.
IV. APPROACH
Our approach to achieve the goals discussed in Section II
comprises  two key components: Visualizer (Module ⃝A in
Figure 1), and Slicing Engine (Module ⃝B in Figure 1).

Initially, when users upload an APK in SliceViz, Visual-
izer’s user interface (A.1) processes user requests and executes
the Slicing Engine (⃝B ) with selected command-line options,
such as specific risk sources, control dependencies, timeouts,
and maximum node  limits  for  slices.  It  then parses  the
Slicing Engine’s output, and visualizes the control and data
dependencies within the sliced source code in Jimple (a Java
intermediate representation [20]) and Java.
A. Visualizer
Visualizer  (⃝A in Figure 1)  is  the entry point  for users,
allowing  them to upload APKs. The user  interface  (A.1)
manages user inputs and interacts with the Slicing Engine ( ⃝B )
by executing the analysis based on selected parameters.
Upon completion of  the slicing process, Visualizer con-
sumes  the  report  in  form of  text  files generated by  the
Slicing Engine,  and displays a summary of  the results on
SliceViz’s dashboard (cf. Figure 3). Each identified program
slice receives two distinct risk ratings.
First,  it  is  classified based on  the  risk of  its  source,
according to the risk categories defined in the identifier dataset
(B.1). Second, it is assigned a warning level using a six-tier
Warning Scale introduced by us (A–F, cf. Table I) based on
the processing operations performed on the privacy-relevant
data, which helps prioritize actions. Levels A and B denote low
risks,  levels C and D represent moderate risks, while levels
E and F indicate high risks requiring immediate attention.
Specific messages accompanying each level provide detailed
insights into the potential risks. For example, since the iden-
tifier dataset categorizes any method collecting device ID as
a  risk 1 source, any program slice originating from a device
ID source will be categorized as  risk 1 .

However, within this
category, the warning level (A–F) may vary depending on the
processing operations in the program slice (cf. Table I).
Users can then select a program slice to view its graphical
visualization in SliceViz (workflow shown in Figure 2).
B. Slicing Engine
Slicing Engine (⃝B in Figure 1) is the back-end of SliceViz,
responsible for performing static analysis on APKs and gener-
ating dependence graphs. It requires two elements, namely an
identifier dataset (B.1) to match system API calls with privacy
labels, and a collection of privacy-relevant libraries package
names to identify privacy-relevant methods (B.2). The Slicing
Engine employs both B.1 and B.2 to identify privacy-relevant
sources, and then slices the Android app from these privacy-
relevant sources. It is implemented as Java code and seeks to
answer two primary questions that developers might have:
1) Which data collected by the app should be considered
and reported as high-risk?
2) How does this data flow through the source code, and
does it flow into data processing methods (eg. analytics,
advertisement, authorization methods)?
B.1 Identifier Dataset. To answer  the first question,  the
Slicing Engine uses a manually annotated identifier dataset
introduced by Khedkar et al. [15], which labels data sources

B.2 Privacy-Relevant Libraries. The Slicing Engine stati-
cally matches the system API calls against a list of packages
of privacy-relevant third-party libraries and APIs. Note that the
Slicing Engine is designed as a proactive privacy by design [5]
measure,  integrating into the app developer’s workflow to
analyze privacy-relevant  third party libraries and API calls
early in the development process.  It operates before name
mangling or obfuscation [51], [52], which are typically applied
later when packaging the app for deployment.
We consulted the AppBrain statistics [53] and related liter-
ature [54]–[56] to select the most commonly used libraries for
pseudonymization,  analytics,  advertisements,  authentication,
network, input/output, email, image processing, data serializa-
tion and location. According to GDPR, robust pseudonymiza-
tion  involves creating pseudonymns  that  cannot  be easily
re-identified and reproduced by third parties [32]. Hashing,
considered a weak technique for pseudonymization, can be re-
versed with a limited dictionary [57].

Grading pseudonymiza-
tion functions based on these criteria can help app developers
identify areas requiring additional data protection measures.
After  the slicing process is complete,  the Slicing Engine
generates text files with privacy-relevant program slices and
transfers them to Visualizer for further analysis.
Instead of high-effort  random sampling, Alice (cf. Sec-
tion II) can now use SliceViz to generate program slices orig-
inating from all privacy-relevant data sources, and visualize.

We now discuss the implementation details of SliceViz
(cf. Figure 1).
A. Visualizer
Users upload an APK in SliceViz, and Visualizer’s user
interface(A.1)processesuserrequestsandexecutestheSlicing
Engine (⃝B) with selected command-line options. The user
interface(A.1)usesD3.js[58],aJavaScriptlibraryforvisual-
izing data. D3.js offers customization, scalability, flexibility
and operates at a low level while creating complex and
dynamic visualization [59].
OncetheSlicingEnginecompletesitsexecution,Visualizer
consumes the text files generated by the Slicing Engine ( ⃝B
in Figure 1). It parses them and visualizes the program slices
asgraphs,renderingkeycomponentssuchasmetadata,nodes,

edges, and other attributes on the screen (A.2).
Users can then select a program slice to view its graphical
visualization in SliceViz (workflow shown in Figure 2). The
visualized graph (cf. Figure 5) allows users to check docu-
mentation of the data source and offers other documentation viability of our approach we conducted two pilot studies

(⃝B) and the Jimple view. For an initial assessment of the tion, those unfamiliar with Jimple struggled to interpret the

Fig. 5: Visualized Jimple slice in Roidsec [48], blue
graphs generated by SliceViz. Additionally, visualizations with
more than 50 nodes became challenging to interpret, due to
increased cognitive load, as proven by Yoghourdjian et al. [60].
Since Jimple breaks down each Java statement into multiple
statements, the Jimple view resulted in larger slices.
To address this, we implemented an experimental Java view
that  relies on the back-end to simplify and transform the
data. The Slicing Engine’s transformers omit Jimple-specific
nodes,  reducing the size and complexity of  the application
dependence graph (ADG) while preserving essential privacy-
relevant information. Developers can now choose which view,
Jimple or Java,  they use (cf. Figure 4). Moreover,  the UI
provides  three key customization options (cf. Figure 4)  to
manage graph complexity and reduce visual overload:
1) Toggle inclusion of control dependencies in the graph.
2) Set a maximum analysis time limit for generating slices.
3) Specify a maximum number of nodes allowed in a graph.
B. Slicing Engine
Slicing Engine (⃝B ) is the back-end of SliceViz, responsible
for performing static analysis on APKs and generating depen-
dence graphs.
The Slicing Engine is a command-line tool  implemented
in Java.  It  is built on top of Jicer  [39],  a static program
slicer that works with Jimple, an intermediate representation
of  Java code.  Jicer  is  tailored to address Android-specific
challenges, such as the absence of a main method, extensive
use of callbacks and inter-component communication [39].
The Slicing Engine uses  Jicer’s  functionality  to statically
construct an application dependence graph (ADG) from the
APK, preserving control and data dependencies in the source
code [61]. It  introduces its own algorithm to match method
signatures from the ADG with the identifier dataset  (B.1),
labeling privacy-relevant  data  sources  in  the code. Once
labeled,  the Slicing Engine uses Jicer’s slicing component
to perform forward slicing on the ADG,  starting from the
identified privacy-relevant data sources. This process yields
forward slices representing the code components reachable
from all  labeled privacy-relevant data sources. Finally,  the
Slicing Engine adds  its own functionality to automatically
label pseudonymization methods and other privacy-relevant

edge: data edge, green edge: control and data edge.
Fig. 6: Java view for the Roidsec Jimple slice (cf. Figure 5).
methods within  the  sliced ADG using a privacy-relevant
libraries dataset (B.2). It stores the sliced ADGs as text files,
facilitating further analysis.
When the Java view is enabled through the user interface
(cf. Figure 4), SliceViz triggers the Slicing Engine to execute
custom transformations on the sliced ADGs. These transfor-
mations  remove  intermediate Jimple-specific nodes,  reduce
graph size and complexity, and convert Jimple nodes into more
readable, Java-like representations to improve comprehension
for developers. The transformed graphs present semantically
correct  Java-like statements  (cf. Figure 6)  instead of  raw
Jimple statements (cf. Figure 5), enabling developers to work
effectively without needing prior Jimple knowledge.
Currently, the Slicing Engine transforms key Jimple state-
ments into Java-like representations, including invoke, assign-
ment,  if, return, binary expressions,  invoke expressions, and
array variables.

These statements are commonly involved in
data flows and their processing, and they tend to be more
complex to interpret  in Jimple compared to Java. Moreover,
recent  research [62]  reveals  that  function calls,  expression
statements, if and decl statements implement privacy behaviors
in Android apps. We are actively working on expanding the
mapping between Jimple and Java to improve the Java view.
Early experiments – Slicing Engine. At  the initial as-
sessment of SliceViz, we also tested the Slicing Engine on
36 applications: 5 apps from DroidBench [63], 25 apps from
TaintBench [48], and 6 real-world Android apps available on
Google Play Store. While DroidBench contains hand-crafted,
small micro benchmark apps, the TaintBench suite consists of.

the program slicing and compared the sizes of slices generated
by default and the slices generated when excluding control
dependencies while building the ADG.
We observed that excluding control dependencies signif-
icantly reduced slice sizes. We thus added a customization
option in the user interface (cf. Figure 4), that allows toggling
inclusion of control dependencies in the graph.
C. Correctness Check
To ensure  the correctness of SliceViz’s  results, we first
conducted sanity checks by manually verifying its output. The
experiments were performed on an Ubuntu 20.04.2 machine
with an Intel(R) Core i7-10850H processor (6 cores).
We tested SliceViz on 32 applications from TaintBench [48],
verifying the presence of detected risk sources against  the
ground truth. We chose all (32) apps from the TaintBench suite
that had code size less than 60,000 lines. The sanity check
revealed that all  the sources detected by SliceViz matched
with the sources present in the TaintBench ground truth. On
average, SliceViz processed each app in 41.96 seconds.
VI. EVALUATION
In Sections  IV and V, we  introduced SliceViz,  a web-
based tool that analyzes an Android app by slicing all privacy-
relevant data sources detected in the app’s source code. Next,
we evaluate SliceViz in a user study to answer the following
research questions:
RQ1. To what extent does SliceViz help developers under-
stand data protection in Android apps?
RQ2. To what extent does the experimental Java view better
support  developers  in understanding  the flow of  privacy-
relevant data within an app?
A. Experimental Setup
To answer the research questions, we conducted a within-
subjects user study [64] with 12 participants. The user study
design was approved by the Ethics committee of our institu-
tion. The 12 participants of this user study are disjoint from
the 16 participants of the pilot studies initially conducted for
SliceViz.  Interested participants were first sent  to a landing
page informing them about  the study’s purpose. Then,  they
were shown our consent form where we informed them about
how we would handle their data, their right to terminate the
study at  any time,  and where  they could give consent  to
participate in the study. Finally, they could schedule select time
slots for the user study. Our consent form, privacy policy and
data handling practices were approved by the Data protection
office of our institution.
All  the participants were Computer Science students. At
the beginning of  the study, participants were given all  the
information about  the study,  and were asked  to sign  the
informed consent  form. They then answered questions on

a Likert  scale from 1 (beginner)  to 5 (expert) about  their
experience (cf. Table II).
Next, participants received a 5-minute overview of SliceViz.
Since we wanted them to try out  the tool  themselves, we
skipped a detailed demo.
Participants were then asked to access SliceViz through a
web server, and complete three tasks:
Task 1: Analyze an app from the micro-benchmark Droid-
Bench [63] by identifying the data collected,  determining
GDPR protection requirements, and observing privacy-relevant
properties  in the resulting program slice. While  the dash-
board provided partial support, fully assessing privacy-relevant
properties required further inspection of the data flow in the
visualized program slices.
Task 2: Perform the same analysis as in Task 1 on a more
complex app from the TaintBench suite [48].
Task 3: Evaluate both Jimple and Java views in terms of
usability, simplicity, and relevance to app-specific tasks.
Tasks 1 and 2 were designed to assess if SliceViz assists
developers in understanding data protection in Android apps
(RQ1). For  these  tasks, DroidBench and TaintBench apps
were chosen because  their  source code and ground  truth
were available. In addition to the documented ground truth,
we manually examined the app source code to evaluate the
accuracy of participant responses.
Task 3 was designed to understand how effectively the
experimental Java view aids in understanding program slices
in comparison to the Jimple view (RQ2). To evaluate the Java
view, participants were given a DroidBench app, and asked to
explore and understand both views.
After  these  tasks,  participants answered several  5-point
Likert-type  questions  from  the  System Usability  Scale
(SUS) [65], a questionnaire designed to measure the effec-
tiveness and efficiency of a system, and rated SliceViz using a
Net Promoter Score (NPS) [66], that measures their likelihood
of recommending the tool to a friend.
B. Results
In this section, we present  the results of  the user study
conducted with 12 participants, who we refer to as P01–P12.
We discarded P05’s data, since they declared themselves to
be a beginner and novice in all skills, and required additional
assistance and time during the study,  far beyond what was
needed by other participants.

We summarize the results below:
Participant Experience. At  the beginning of  the study,
participants self-reported familiarity with Android app de-
velopment (median 1/5), Java (median 3/5), Jimple (median
2/5),  static analysis  tools (median 2/5), graph visualization
(median 3/5), data privacy (median 3/5), and GDPR (median
3/5) (cf. Table II). Participants have a median of approximately
1.5 years of experience in Java development.
RQ1. To what  extent does SliceViz help developers
understand data protection in Android apps?
Participants first worked with a DroidBench app, which had
one privacy-relevant program slice with 10 nodes. Despite

limited knowledge of GDPR, 10 participants successfully iden-
tified the privacy risks in the app and accurately determined
which data required GDPR protection. Apart from P04 who
over-reported the risky sources, all the other participants were
able to analyze the slices, interpret the graphs, and understand
the data flow, correctly identifying privacy-relevant properties.
The TaintBench app given for Task 2 had seven pro-
gram slices, with a maximum size of 20 nodes. Of  the 11
participants, 10 accurately identified all  the privacy-relevant
information in the given app. P03 correctly identified 50%
of  the privacy-relevant details,  but  failed to report  risk 4
slices, likely due to customizing SliceViz to only report risk 1
data sources. Interestingly, P04 (who struggled to understand
privacy risks and data flow in the first task) could successfully
identify and report the privacy-relevant details in this app.
The first two tasks included questions about the dashboard.
Only P02 felt they could understand the slices without using
the dashboard, likely due to their advanced Android develop-
ment experience (cf. Table II). They suggested more detailed
description of all data sources within the dashboard. All 11
participants agreed that  the overview and the hints provided
by  the dashboard helped  them better understand  the  tool
(cf. Figure 7).
Four participants (P03, P07, P10, and P12) explicitly men-
tioned the warning scale as one of the most useful features of
the dashboard. P03 noted, “I like how all risks are categorized
with source and warning scale”. P10 said, “Warning scale
is the best way to convey overall risks”. P04 found the bar
and pie charts easy to understand. P09 said, “SliceViz gave
me a good understanding about privacy-relevant data flows.
Warning scale was helpful. I prioritized looking at slices rated
D before I moved on to safer slices”. P12 confessed that
SliceViz made them aware of potential privacy risks.
Using the System Usability Scale (SUS), SliceViz obtained
a mean score of 63.96, which falls below the industry bench-
mark of 68. Notably, users with no prior Java development
experience expressed dissatisfaction with its usability. Since
SliceViz is designed for privacy analysis and visualization,
the gap in experience with Android development and static
analysis tools (cf. Table II) could explain some of the usability

challenges reported by less experienced users.
However, participants with one to five years of Java de-
velopment experience rated SliceViz with an average SUS
score of 68, indicating a satisfactory level of usability. These
findings suggest that SliceViz provides an acceptable usability
experience for Java and Android developers.
The Net Promoter Score (NPS) for SliceViz was calculated
as 18.18, reflecting a positive perception among participants.
RQ1. SliceViz effectively assists developers with prior
development  experience  in  identifying and observing
privacy-relevant properties in Android apps.
RQ2. To what extent does the experimental Java view
better support developers  in understanding the flow of
privacy-relevant data within an app?
Most of the participants (N=10) agreed that the Java view
was easier and faster to understand compared to the Jimple
view: “I have more experience with Java so I could quickly
understand the Java view” (P07), “Unlike Jimple view, Java
view shows the data of risk sources in a simple way instead
of giving too many details.” (P02). While P09 also found
the Java view quicker to grasp,  they appreciated the Jimple
view for its detailed control flow information. However, they
acknowledged that their familiarity with Jimple made it easier
to  follow,  and  those  less  experienced with Jimple might
struggle with its unfamiliar syntax and temporary variables.
Notably, all 11 participants agreed or strongly agreed that they
were more comfortable with Java view (cf.

Figure 7).
To compare user preferences for both views, participants
were asked, “How likely is  it you would recommend Java
view over Jimple view to a friend?” (and vice versa). Their
responses were used to calculate separate Net Promoter Scores
(NPS)  for each view. The NPS for Java view was 45.45,
reflecting strong user preference over  the Jimple view.  In
contrast, the Jimple view received a negative score of -72.73
(individual ratings in Figure 8).
Participants found the Jimple view complex (P06: “The
Jimple view has  too many nodes and edges”),  hard  to
understand, and not user-friendly for beginners (P02: “It is.

We next discuss the limitations of our approach.
Slicing Engine. The identifier dataset may miss out on
some libraries. However, Khedkar et al. [15] ensure a com-
prehensive evaluation by including the most used third-party
libraries from different categories. The degree of identifiability
relies on human subjectivity and it may vary with different
domains and compliance regimes. Since our approach focuses
on GDPR, we can use the identifier dataset directly. The
Slicing Engine matches the method signatures in the ADG
with the labeled methods present  in the identifier dataset.
Since we use keyword matching to detect  the presence of
data sources including those from some third-party libraries,
the analysis cannot handle code obfuscation in cases where
the obfuscation also renames calls to these third-party APIs.
However, as mentioned in Section V,  the Slicing Engine is
designed to operate as a proactive privacy measure before
name mangling or obfuscation measures are applied. The Java
view can be improved by expanding the mapping between
Jimple and Java, comprehensively covering the app source
code.
Usability Assessment. To  investigate possible usability
implications of SliceViz, we conducted two pilot studies and
one user study. Since it is challenging to recruit developers for
a user study and students are considered reasonable proxies for
software developers [67]–[69], our user study used students as
participants. We conducted the user study in a controlled en-
vironment (12 participants, 1 hour study duration) rather than
in a development setting. In practice, users would have more
time to investigate more advanced apps.  It would, however,
be interesting to conduct a larger quantitative evaluation in
real-life conditions.

In this paper, we have explored how static program slicing
can be used to identify parts of the source code affected by
sources of privacy-relevant data.
To achieve this, we have introduced SliceViz, a web tool that
analyzes an Android app by slicing all privacy-relevant data
sources detected in the source code. It then helps developers
by visualizing these privacy-relevant program slices.
With a user study conducted on 12 participants, we have
shown that SliceViz effectively assists developers with prior
development experience in identifying and observing privacy-
relevant properties in Android apps.
Our findings suggest that program slicing can be employed
to reason about privacy-relevant data flows in Android appli-
cations. With further usability improvements, developers can
be better equipped to handle privacy-sensitive information.
